title: Dog Vomiting Bright Green Liquid: Causes and Emergency Guide
meta_desc: Seeing bright green liquid when your dog vomits? Learn why this is a high-priority emergency and what immediate steps you need to take.
content_html:
TL;DR: Bright green or neon vomit in dogs is a high-priority medical emergency that may indicate toxin ingestion, such as rat poison, or a serious internal blockage. You should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately and take a photo of the vomit to help the clinical team prioritize your dog’s care.

What does it mean if my dog is vomiting bright green liquid?

When a dog vomits bright green liquid, it usually indicates the presence of bile, a digestive fluid produced by the liver. However, if the color is exceptionally vibrant or neon, it may be a sign that your dog has ingested something dangerous, such as rodenticide (rat poison) or items containing industrial dyes. It is important to distinguish between simple grass-stained vomit and a true liquid discharge.

Is bright green vomit in dogs a medical emergency?

  • Bright green vomit is considered a high-priority emergency often linked to life-threatening conditions like toxin ingestion, gallbladder issues, or gastrointestinal blockages.
  • Certain poisons can act rapidly on the central nervous system or interfere with blood clotting abilities.
  • You should contact an emergency veterinarian or a pet poison control center immediately.

How does taking a photo of the vomit help my veterinarian?

  • The exact shade—whether forest green, neon, or yellowish-green—provides critical clues to the source of the illness.
  • A photo allows the vet to evaluate consistency and check for any fragments of bait or packaging.
  • Visual evidence helps the veterinary team prioritize your dog's treatment the moment you arrive at the clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

The sudden onset of vomiting in dogs, potentially with bile, may stem from dietary indiscretion (ingestion of novel, spoiled, or contaminated foods), drug or toxin ingestion (antibiotics, NSAIDs, corticosteroids, plants, chemicals), systemic illness (pancreatitis, uremic gastropathy, hypoadrenocorticism), endoparasitism (Physaloptera sp), or bacterial (Helicobacter-associated disease) or viral infections (canine parvovirus). Diagnostic efforts should intensify if foreign objects or toxins were accessible, clinical signs persist beyond 2 days of symptomatic treatment, hematemesis or melena are present, the animal is systemically unwell, or abdominal palpation reveals abnormalities. Diagnostic imaging, including plain or barium contrast radiographs, may be warranted.

Chapter: Gastroenterology, Toxicology, Infectious Disease

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 387)

faqs.question: Could it just be grass?
faqs.answer: While grass can turn vomit green, it usually contains visible blades of grass and a more organic scent. Bright, neon, or purely liquid green vomit is much more likely to be bile or a chemical toxin.

faqs.question: Is it okay to wait until my regular vet opens?
faqs.answer: No. Because bright green vomit can indicate the ingestion of fast-acting toxins like rat poison, waiting even a few hours can be fatal. Seek emergency care immediately.

faqs.question: What should I tell the emergency vet on the phone?
faqs.answer: Tell them the exact color, when the vomiting started, any other symptoms like lethargy or shaking, and if you have any known poisons or chemicals in your home or yard.
